data.title: Bowser says she appreciates city's patience as DC struggles to dig out of snow
data.description:
Washington, D.C., Mayor Muriel Bowser (D) on Tuesday thanked residents for dealing with the District’s response to Winter Storm Fern, which brought about 7 inches of snow to parts of the nation’s capital. 

“This winter storm was complex, and our response tactics have had to evolve. Ice, sleet, and snow—combined with persistent, below-freezing temperatures—have made clearing efforts particularly difficult,” Bowser wrote in a post on the social platform X.

“Most main roads in DC are now passable. Side and residential roads are our focus. DC, we appreciate your patience—our teams will be out until the work is done,” she added.

Residents have struggled to walk on sidewalks, drive on roads and take a scooter to local metro stops amid the freezing conditions. Federal offices were shuttered on Monday and employees were advised by the Office of Personnel Management to work from home.

The Metro also delayed bus service on Monday with drivers starting their routes at 8 a.m. EST.

In total, Reagan National Airport, located right outside of D.C. in Arlington, Va., recorded more than 6 inches of snow, according to local outlet Fox 5.

Dulles International Airport in Chantilly, Va., saw 7 inches while Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport faced more than 11 inches of snowfall, per the outlet.

The harsh weather halted travelers nationwide with about 12,000 delays and 4,600 cancellations as of Monday morning, according to FlightAware.

Passengers were sidelined by major airlines including Delta, American and Southwest.

data.title: Epstein files expected to be released 'in the near term': DOJ
data.description:
The Department of Justice (DOJ) on Tuesday told federal judges that they expect to release additional files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein “in the near term.”

Attorney General Pam Bondi, Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche and U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York Jay Clayton provided the update in a court filing to Judges Richard Berman and Paul Engelmayer of the Southern District of New York, adding that they could not provide an exact date due to the “ongoing process” of “quality control checks” and identifying names of victims to be redacted.

The DOJ reviewed “millions” of documents, which the department said was a “substantial undertaking” given the amount of documents to review and because “the capabilities of the document management systems are not in themselves sufficient to ensure victim identifying information is redacted,” the court filing reads.

Reviewing the documents required electronically and manually searching for “hundreds of self-identified victims as well as individuals, out of an abundance of caution, the Department itself believes are or may be victims based on the information available to the Department,” DOJ leaders wrote. The DOJ also worked with victims and their lawyers “to ensure that the anticipated productions identify and redact victim-identifying information,” in compliance with the Epstein Files Transparency Act.

The court filing adds that DOJ “are working diligently to complete this review as expeditiously as possible without compromising victims’ privacy so that its production can be complete.”

The DOJ’s filed on Epstein are comprised of photos, notes, video, audio and other documents. The files were set to all be released 30 days after President Trump signed the Epstein Files Transparency Act into law on Dec. 19. A portal was launched on the DOJ’s website, with a webpage called the “Epstein Library.”

Documents were slowly released in batches on Dec. 19 and in some of the days that followed, drawing outrage from the bill’s sponsors, Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), along with other lawmakers. Massie accused Bondi of “grossly” violating the deadline in releasing all the files, with the partial release showing several heavily redacted documents.

“Unfortunately, today’s document release by @AGPamBondi and @DAGToddBlanche grossly fails to comply with both the spirit and the letter of the law that @realDonaldTrump signed just 30 days ago,” Massie posted on the social platform X on Dec. 19. 

Massie and Khanna threatened to hold Bondi in “inherent contempt” over the partial release. They requested Engelmayer’s approval to appoint a special master to oversee the DOJ’s work to release the documents and add pressure on the department, but last week Engelmayer denied their request.

Blanche said earlier this month that the DOJ had over 2 million documents to review, though Reuters reported that 5.2 million documents were still in need of review.

Americans have largely disapproved of the DOJ’s handling of the Epstein files, with 6 percent saying they were satisfied, according to a CNN survey released last week.

data.title: The silence of the 'Don't Tread on Me' crowd is deafening on ICE
data.description:
When I was a teen, I watched as federal agents, dressed up like commandos, tried to storm a religious compound in Texas. A shootout and then a siege ensued in which the government used the same psyops operations on Americans as they had on Panamanian Gen. Manuel Noriega. Fifty-one days later, I watched the government breach the compound, and watched a fire break out. Scores of Americans died, including dozens of children.

I’m not here to absolve David Koresh or the Branch Davidians of any sins. But as a teen, I was baffled by the government’s actions and willingness to escalate things to the point of using commando-style tactics before exhausting other options. Because of this, I would understand why people didn’t trust the government, why they advocated for the Second Amendment, and why they warned me about the dangers that an unchecked politician could do to American citizens.

And I took to it. As a Marine, gun owner and patriotic American I held fast to that belief that we are a country of law and order but we also have every right not to be bullied by the government. As the line from “V for Vendetta” goes, “People should not be afraid of their government. Governments should be afraid of their people.”

And yet, time and again, I have seen self-described libertarians, Second Amendment advocates, Punisher logo wearing tough guys, and “Don’t Tread on Me” flag wavers wilt like flowers when it comes time to actually standing up for the Bill of Rights. It seems that many of these individuals were actually OK with rights being trampled as long as it wasn’t them. I guess “Don’t Tread on Me” was meant to be taken selfishly and not applied to every other citizen in this country.

I watched as these people stayed silent when Philando Castile was killed for having a legal weapon, when Tamir Rice was killed for holding a toy gun in an open carry state, when John Crawford was killed for holding a toy gun inside the Wal-Mart that sold the gun. I have watched these people make excuses for violations of American civil rights. “Don’t’ Tread on Me” was replaced by “comply or die” when it came to people that weren’t the race, religion, political party, or any other identity they hated.

It was beyond ludicrous to see people claim that you should do everything the police tell you to do, even if the police were wrong, justify wrongful killings and then see people assault police officers at the U.S. Capitol. And in this past horrid year, claim that some people didn’t need due process, or that there are tiers to due process, or that due process is subjective, or that maybe it just shouldn’t exist.

Which leads us to Minnesota. Yet another citizen was shot and killed by federal immigration enforcement agents. The Department of Homeland Security immediately tried to control the messaging (because it’s better to be first than right nowadays) that somehow this man who was legally permitted to carry a gun was killed for carrying a gun. Before people could even process this tragedy, they proclaimed he was brandishing the weapon. I think about all the “Don’t Tread on Me” people and wonder, why are they so silent?

Or why are some putting restrictions on the Second Amendment now? You can carry a gun but not magazines? You can’t carry more than one magazine? You can’t bring a gun to a protest if you are a Democrat? It seems that a lot of people that preach about a tyrannical government seem to think that protesting your government is worthy of a death sentence. Gun rights organizations like the NRA have pushed back on the Trump administration’s stance, but will that carry over to their members or MAGA voters?

There is nothing normal or laudable about ICE’s actions in Minnesota or any other place in America. They have trampled on due process, several amendments of the Bill of Rights, and state laws that are meant to protect citizens from tyranny.

Over the years, I was told by my conservative friends to be worried about Big Government. A lot of my Marine buddies rightly told me that the billions we spend on weapons could be used on us if the wrong person wanted to do it. Like many gun owners, I want to see school shootings and street violence end but also realize I have a right to protect myself from the government.

But I guess none of that applies anymore. The killings of Alex Pretti, Renee Good and others in ICE custody should be reprehensible to any decent, patriotic American. But the silence is deafening from those who cried loudest over government tyranny. The lessons of Waco were that the government needed to be scared of us. But it seems that the “Don’t Tread on Me” crowd is instead scared of the government. That must be the reason they are so quiet right now … right?

Jos Joseph is a recipient of the Military Reporters and Editors award for Best Commentary / Opinion. A graduate of Harvard and Ohio State, he is a Marine veteran who served in Iraq. He currently lives in Anaheim, Calif.

data.title: Record-breaking South Carolina measles outbreak nearing 800 cases
data.description:
The record-breaking measles outbreak in South Carolina has reached nearly 800 cases, making it the largest spread of the disease in the U.S. since it was first declared eliminated at the start of the century.

The South Carolina Department of Health on Tuesday reported 89 new measles cases since Friday, bringing the current outbreak total to 789. That figure surpasses the 762 cases confirmed in West Texas last year.

Health officials in the Palmetto State said 557 people are still in quarantine until Feb. 19, and 20 people are in isolation.

The outbreak, which began last September, has been concentrated in South Carolina’s Upstate region, where nearly 90 percent of those who are sick are children under the age of 17. Students at nearly two dozen schools have been quarantined due to measles exposure.

The number of cases held somewhat steady through last fall but quickly accelerated over the holidays, with a state dashboard showing cases jumped from 181 cases on Dec. 21 to 434 cases by Jan. 4.

Most cases, 695, are in unvaccinated individuals, according to health officials. Another 14 cases are in individuals who have received one of the recommended two-dose MMR vaccine, 20 cases are in already-vaccinated individuals. The vaccination status of individuals in 60 cases is unknown.

Health officials noted that at least 18 people, including adults and children, have been hospitalized for complications since the start of the outbreak; however, patients are not required to report that information to the agency.

The benchmark in South Carolina comes as the U.S. is on the verge of losing its measles elimination status after more than two decades.

A country technically loses the status after 12 months of consistent spread of the disease, a milestone the U.S. hit on Jan. 20. However, Ralph Abraham, a former Louisiana surgeon general who was sworn in as the Center for Disease Control and Prevention’s (CDC) principal deputy director in December, said it was too soon to make a determination.

“The United States would lose the elimination status when a chain of transmission — and those are important words too — last for 12 months or more. Now, what is the chain of transmission? It’s defined as all cases linked to a common source through an epidemic linkage. So those things matter,” Abraham said, dismissing the possibility as the “cost of doing business.”

Experts have previously drawn a connection between the anti-vaccine rhetoric coming from Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. amid the spread of measles. Kennedy has long promoted the scientifically unproven claim that vaccines, including the MMR vaccine, are linked to autism in children.

“This is a very clear example of the damage that the anti-vaccine movement has done in the United States,” Fiona Havers, adjunct associate professor at the Emory School of Medicine and a former infectious disease staffer at the CDC, told The Hill last December.

“There are a number of things that have made these ongoing outbreaks very difficult to control. One is that the decades of false information about measles vaccines that [Kennedy Jr.] and other people in the anti-vaccine movement have been spreading has led to a decline in vaccination rates,” she added.

data.title: Amazon cutting 16,000 jobs as it ramps up AI push
data.description:
Amazon is axing 16,000 jobs as it ramps up a push to use artificial intelligence (AI) in the workplace.

“The reductions we are making today will impact approximately 16,000 roles across Amazon, and we’re again working hard to support everyone whose role is impacted,” Amazon’s senior vice president of people experience and technology Beth Galetti said in a Wednesday message to employees.

“That starts with offering most US-based employees 90 days to look for a new role internally (timing will vary internationally based on local and country level requirements),” she added.

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the Tipsheet newsletter

Amazon CEO Andy Jassy said in June he expected the company’s workforce to shrink in the coming years as it uses more AI. Jassy laid out how Amazon was launching more generative AI and agents for internal operations, saying the employment of the tools should “change the way our work is done.”

“We will need fewer people doing some of the jobs that are being done today, and more people doing other types of jobs,” Jassy told employees at the time.

“It’s hard to know exactly where this nets out over time, but in the next few years, we expect that this will reduce our total corporate workforce as we get efficiency gains from using AI extensively across the company.”

Wednesday’s announcement comes after Amazon said in October that it would eliminate another about 14,000 jobs.

data.title: Trump endorses GOP rep in Wisconsin governor race
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday endorsed Rep. Tom Tiffany (R-Wis.) in the open race for governor of battleground Wisconsin. 

Trump, in a lengthy post on Truth Social, said the lawmaker has “always been at my side” and gave him a “Complete and Total” endorsement as he touted his own 2024 win in Wisconsin. 

“I endorsed Tom for Congress early and strong, and he won, BIG. Likewise, he has always been at my side. A very successful Businessman, Family Farmer, and State Legislator, prior to becoming a distinguished United States Congressman, Tom is a Proven Leader who has dedicated his life to serving his Community,” the president wrote.

The president added that Tiffany will work to grow the economy, support border security, and “protect our always under siege Second Amendment,” among other priorities to “advance Common Sense Values.”

The backing helps cement the Republican lawmaker as frontrunner of the August GOP primary for the seat, which Cook Political Report rates as a toss up.

Tiffany is vying to succeed Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers (D), who announced last year that he would not seek a third term, kicking off the state’s first open race for governor in over a decade.  

Across the aisle, more than half a dozen Democrats are competing for their party’s nomination in the governor’s race, including former Wisconsin Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes and current Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez (D).

Evers was reelected in 2022 by roughly 3 points — but Trump flipped the state in the 2024 presidential race, winning by less than a percentage point.

Trump on Tuesday also endorsed Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy’s son-in-law, Michael Alfonso, in the GOP primary to succeed Tiffany, who is leaving his House seat to run for governor.

data.title: Live updates Trump promotes his 'Trump accounts;' Senate tackles funding package amid DHS furor
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday is speaking at a Treasury Department summit, where he will promote his investment accounts for newborn babies.

Meanwhile, on Capitol Hill, the Senate is working to advance a six-bill spending package, despite calls from Democrats to separate out Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the deadly weekend shooting of a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis by federal immigration officials.

The incident has put Republicans on defense, and they are starting to splinter over support for DHS Secretary Kristi Noem. She retains the confidence of President Trump, however.

Overnight, Minnesota Rep. Ilhan Omar (D), who represents Minneapolis and some of its suburbs, was attacked at town hall in the city, as she called for Noem’s impeachment.

This morning, Secretary of State Marco Rubio is testifying before the Senate on the administration’s actions in Venezuela. Watch live here. 

Later, the Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision and chair Jerome Powell will deliver remarks. The central bank is expected to keep rates steady.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump plans to cut FEMA workforce in half, court filing alleges
data.description:
The Trump administration is advancing plans to cut down the workforce at the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), unions and public interest groups alleged in court.

In filing on Tuesday night, the organizations said that the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which oversees FEMA, could eliminate more than 10,000 positions in the months ahead.

The court filing said that FEMA management was directed to plan for a 50 percent staffing cut and that a spreadsheet attached to the email called for a 15 percent cut for “Permanent Fulltime” staff, a 41 percent for “Disaster Fulltime” staff and an 85 percent cut in“Surge Workforce.”

CNN previously reported on the spreadsheet. At that time, a spokesperson for DHS said that the target numbers were included in error and that “numerical assumptions reflected in that draft were not approved, were not adopted, and do not represent FEMA policy or leadership direction.” The Hill has reached out to DHS and FEMA for comment.

The court filing, meanwhile, alleges that staffing reductions are already underway at FEMA. It said that on New Year’s Eve, some employees that are part of FEMA’s Cadre of On-Call Response/Recovery Employees (CORE) received notices that their positions would not be renewed.

While just 65 people received these notices, between 900 and 1,000 employees have renewal dates in January.

It said that non-renewals of these employees are not routine.

The groups ask the court to block DHS from imposing the 50 percent reduction target and prevent it from taking away FEMA’s authority not to renew the workforce.

“Gutting the staff responsible for disaster preparedness and response does not make the country safer; it leaves families, local governments, and first responders without the support they rely on when emergencies strike. The law does not permit the executive branch to dismantle FEMA in this way, and this lawsuit seeks to ensure the agency can do the job Congress created it to do,” said a written statement from Skye Perryman, president and CEO of Democracy Forward, which is representing some of the plaintiffs.

The Trump administration has repeatedly said it wants to either dismantle or reform FEMA. It was supposed to hold a meeting to outline its plans to do so last month, but it was abruptly canceled, leaving the public in the dark about what the agency is planning, if anything.

The filing is part of a broader case related to the status of federal employees as the Trump administration has sought to shrink the size of the federal workforce. 

A stopgap funding bill prevented the administration from laying off employees. That bill is set to lapse soon.

data.title: Graham to protesters in Iran: 'Help will be on the way'
data.description:
Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) assured Iranian protesters of U.S. support on Tuesday.

“To the Iranian people, President Trump has your back, help will be on the way,” Graham said in an interview with Fox News on Tuesday. “I’m not gonna say any more.”

His statement on Fox News echoes President Trump’s comments earlier this month that “help is on the way” for anti-regime demonstrators. The U.S. did not intervene and over 6,000 people have been killed during the protests, according to activist reports. 

The protests began in late December and were spurred by a drastic drop in the value of the country’s currency. 

The arrival of the USS Abraham Lincoln aircraft carrier and American warships in the Middle East earlier this week signaled a readiness from the U.S. to intervene in the conflict if deemed necessary. 

Graham told Fox News that Trump is an “eye-for-an-eye guy,” citing his administration’s recent removal of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro. 

Amid the fleet’s travel to the Middle East last week, Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One, “I’d rather not see anything happen, but we’re watching them very closely.”

On Saturday, Iranian Revolutionary Guard Cmdr. Mohammad Pakpour said the country’s military has its “finger on the trigger” in case the U.S. decides to respond.

Graham criticized Iranian Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s regime as “religious Nazis,” saying, “They’re drunk on religion.” 

“The Nazis wanted a master race, the Ayatollah wants a master religion, where we’ll bow to it,” the South Carolina senator said. “Khamenei’s killing his people, but I think he’ll be out of the killing people pretty soon business.

“I think a new day is coming for Iran,” Graham continued. “I see Iran down the road could be a friend for America, but the Ayatollah is evil and Donald Trump is pretty good at standing up to evil.”

data.title: Graham to Republicans: Stephen Miller is not the problem
data.description:
Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), a close ally of President Trump, is arguing to Republican colleagues who are critical of Stephen Miller that the senior White House adviser “is not the problem” and will be in Trump’s inner circle until the end of his administration.

“As to Stephen Miller, to my colleagues who believe you can convince Donald Trump that Stephen Miller is a liability for him, good luck with that. When this clock strikes 12 on the Trump era, there will be a few people walking out the door with Donald Trump. Stephen Miller will be in that group,” Graham said in a Fox News interview.

“So I want everybody to know on the Republican side that Stephen Miller is not the problem, sanctuary cities is the problem,” Graham said.

Graham’s comments were directed at GOP colleagues such as Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) and Sen. Rand Paul (Ky.), who have criticized Miller’s influence over Trump.

Tillis on Tuesday criticized Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem and Miller for claiming that Alex Pretti, the 37-year-old nurse who was fatally shot in Minneapolis over the weekend, was a “domestic terrorist,” despite a lack of evidence to support their claims.

“Those two people told the president before they even had an incident report whatsoever that the person who died was a terrorist. That is amateur hour at its worst,” Tillis said about Noem and Miller.

Tillis also slammed Miller on the Senate floor earlier this month for asserting that the United States has a right to Greenland and that no country would pose a military challenge if the Trump administration took control of the vast mineral-rich territory.

“Mr. Miller said that [Greenland] should be part of the U.S. That is absurd,” Tillis declared on the Senate floor Jan. 7.

“I think it was an amateurish comment and something that a deputy chief of staff and senior policy adviser should not have taken the position on,” he added. “He doesn’t speak for the U.S. government.

Miller immediately labeled Pretti a “domestic terrorist” but didn’t provide any evidence to justify the claim.

In a social media post, he called Pretti a “would-be assassin” who “tried to murder federal law enforcement.”  

Paul, the chairman of the Senate Homeland Security Committee, questioned the rush to characterize Pretti before a full investigation could be completed and noted that most law enforcement chiefs wait for all the facts before drawing conclusions.

“I can’t recall ever hearing a police chief immediately describing the victim as a ‘domestic terrorist’ or a ‘would-be assassin.’ For calm to be restored, an independent investigation is the least that should be done,” Paul wrote, making a pointed reference to Noem and Miller.
